coredata.dc:title: Mechanism insights for formation of graphene quantum dots from Styrofoam waste and its application as security ink
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Dheeraj
Tom Mathew, Blesson
Rani, Sweety
Kumar, Advitiya
Nandan, Bhanu
Srivastava, Rajiv K.
coredata.dc:description: Plastic pollution free environment is the utmost demand of the present day, which arises due to the urgent need of transforming plastic waste into valued materials suitable for wide range of applications. In this context, a single step synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from plastic waste might be a promising approach for bulk conversion of plastic waste into value-added high-end products. The acid/base free, cost-effective, rapid synthesis of GQDs has the potential to meet the future demands. More importantly, a crucial aspect lies in comprehending the underlying mechanism for the formation of such nanomaterials which can open new paths for optimization and functionalization, ultimately facilitating the synthesis of cutting edge materials. This study presents an investigation into the synthesis mechanism of GQDs from Styrofoam waste using the microwave pyrolysis method. The research approach combines experimental technique with simulation studies to elucidate the process. The simulation methodology demonstrated the conversion of Styrofoam into a mix of saturated and unsaturated cyclic structures. These structures subsequently interacted to form intermediate species featuring two or more units bonded together. The intermediate species then gave rise to larger graphene like structure which ultimately led to the formation of GQDs. The GQDs synthesized in this study were subsequently transformed into a printable ink formulation in order to evaluate its potential as a practical solution for addressing the concerns associated with counterfeit currencies, fake documents, and tampered or manipulated goods. The synthesis of GQDs based printable ink from nonbiodegradable plastic waste serves the dual benefit of managing plastic waste and its utilization in advanced applications. This approach holds significant promise in providing sustainable solutions for the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Molecular dynamic simulation
Security ink
Mechanism
Plastic waste

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evolution of cyclic structure from polystyrene
Formation of GQDs from cyclic structure

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by the Indian Institute of Technology Delhi and the Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India (Dy. No. C/5142/IFD) to perform this research work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of synthesized GQDs sample. (B) Raman spectrum of GQDs.
(A) FTIR spectra of Styrofoam waste and synthesized GQDs sample. (B) XPS full scan survey of GQDs with high resolution C1s spectrum inserted at top-left corner.
Three-dimensional model of polystyrene molecule with (A) three and (B) five degrees of polymerization.

(A) Polystyrene molecule degradation into cyclic ring structure (shown in red color) without hydrogen atoms. (B) Different cyclic ring structures formed by changing the random number associated with velocity. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) and (C) cyclic structres taken in simulation box, with and without hydrogen atoms respectively. (B) and (D) GQDs structure formation after the simulation from cyclic structure with and without hydrogen atoms respectively.
GQDs formation, presence of cyclic structure, and formation of intermediate species with respect to time from cyclic structure (A) with and (B) without hydrogen atoms.
GQDs based secured ink printed on a white paper (A) under normal light (B) under UV light.
Proposed synthesis mechanism of GQDs based on simulation study.

head.ce:title: Mechanism insights for formation of graphene quantum dots from Styrofoam waste and its application as security ink

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Textile and Fibre Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Delhi, Hauz Khas, New Delhi 110016, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Textile and Fibre Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Textile and Fibre Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Delhi, Hauz Khas, New Delhi 110016, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Plastic pollution free environment is the utmost demand of the present day, which arises due to the urgent need of transforming plastic waste into valued materials suitable for wide range of applications. In this context, a single step synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from plastic waste might be a promising approach for bulk conversion of plastic waste into value-added high-end products. The acid/base free, cost-effective, rapid synthesis of GQDs has the potential to meet the future demands. More importantly, a crucial aspect lies in comprehending the underlying mechanism for the formation of such nanomaterials which can open new paths for optimization and functionalization, ultimately facilitating the synthesis of cutting edge materials. This study presents an investigation into the synthesis mechanism of GQDs from Styrofoam waste using the microwave pyrolysis method. The research approach combines experimental technique with simulation studies to elucidate the process. The simulation methodology demonstrated the conversion of Styrofoam into a mix of saturated and unsaturated cyclic structures. These structures subsequently interacted to form intermediate species featuring two or more units bonded together. The intermediate species then gave rise to larger graphene like structure which ultimately led to the formation of GQDs. The GQDs synthesized in this study were subsequently transformed into a printable ink formulation in order to evaluate its potential as a practical solution for addressing the concerns associated with counterfeit currencies, fake documents, and tampered or manipulated goods. The synthesis of GQDs based printable ink from nonbiodegradable plastic waste serves the dual benefit of managing plastic waste and its utilization in advanced applications. This approach holds significant promise in providing sustainable solutions for the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials are known for their exceptional properties and promise a better future in almost every field of applied science. The bottom-up and top-down are the two broad categories for nanomaterial synthesis. The bottom-up synthesis technique includes the interaction of atoms or ions of a precursor and reducing agent to form nanomaterial, commonly in the presence of a stabilising agent. On the other hand, the top-down approach involves breaking down giant molecules into nano-range. Pyrolysis, sputtering, chemical etching, ball milling and laser ablation are some techniques used in the top-down approach. The pyrolysis synthesis technique has evolved much in recent times as mass-scale production, minimum steps, easy-to-use, and better size control are some primary advantages of this technology. The pyrolysis technique is widely used to convert plastic and biomass waste into value-added carbon nano-forms.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are the most intriguing among all the carbon nano-forms because of their unique properties, zero-dimensional size and several potential applications. The synthesis of GQDs via the pyrolysis method has been reported using various agricultural wastes like fruits [1–3], nuts [4], vegetables [5–7], food waste [8,9] and stubble [10–12]. Interestingly, nonbiodegradable plastic waste has also been explored for synthesising GQDs via the pyrolysis technique. Multi-step methods involving acid/base pyrolysis for the synthesis of GQDs using polypropylene, polyethylene, polyethylene terephthalate waste has already been reported [13–16]. Recently, we have reported a single step acid/base free synthesis method of GQDs by using a novel precursor [17]. Briefly, Styrofoam waste was pyrolyzed in the microwave without using any acid/base to convert it into nonpolar GQDs. The pyrolysis was performed at specific temperature and exposure time range. GQDs with varying degree of purity was observed at different exposure time. As confirmed in our previous publication, the absence of any hydroxyl or carboxyl group lead to the nonpolar nature of GQDs. The synthesis mechanism of a graphene-like zero-dimensional structure from polystyrene is also crucial for the fundamental understanding of the formation mechanism.

The theoretically proposed formation mechanism of GQDs from graphene [18], graphene oxide [19,20], graphite [21,22], CNTs [23,24], glucose [25], and citric acid [26] has been reported previously. The formation mechanism from biowastes like spent tea [27], rice [28,29], neem leaf [30], bio-refinery waste [31], sea animals [32] and other biowastes [33,34] has also been reported. One of the best way for understanding properties, mechanism, and complex behaviours for an atom, molecular or system is simulation method. But at the same time, performing complex simulations in an isolated box is also challenging as reaction might involve too many reactants, complex molecules, and different environmental conditions. This complexity leads to only handful of reports on simulated synthesis mechanism of GQDs. The formation mechanism of GQDs by using molecular dynamic (MD) simulation based on reactive force field (ReaxFF) has been reported by using CNTs [35] and Kapton molecule [36] as precursor via laser ablation technique at different temperatures. There are not much further information available related to synthesis mechanism of GQDs by any other precursor or methods.

In this work, the formation mechanism of GQDs from Styrofoam waste were studied by ReaxFF based LAMMPS (large-scale atomic/molecular massively parallel simulator) simulation and compared with the experimental studies. During microwave pyrolysis in our previous study, the GQDs of purity 100 % was obtained from Styrofoam (polystyrene) waste at a pyrolysis temperature of 390 °C [17]. Hence in the current ReaxFF simulation study, a polystyrene molecule with DP (degree of polymerisation) of 3 was taken in a three-dimensional isolated box with periodic boundaries. The temperature of the simulation box was kept at 660 K with a damping factor value of 10–100 fs and timestep of 0.1–1.5 fs. The MD simulation was fixed with constant number of atoms, volume and temperature i.e. NVT ensemble. The performed simulation demonstrated the bond cleavage and bond formation in polystyrene molecule under the set temperature conditions and led to better understanding of fundamentals related to formation mechanism of GQDs from polystyrene. Furthermore, the green fluorescent of GQDs showed under ultraviolet (UV) light as shown in Fig. S1, was used to apply GQDs as security inks. The GQDs sample was printed on a white paper using an inkjet printer and examined under visible and UV light. The suitability of low-cost synthesis of security ink form plastic waste possessing uncompromised security features while ensuring sustainability was studied.
Method section include GQDs synthesis experiment, computational setup up and settings used in the simulations to unwrap the synthesis mechanism of GQDs from polystyrene.

Styrofoam waste was collected form refrigerator packaging material. Weight-average (Mw) and number-average (Mn) molecular weight of Styrofoam waste were characterized as 108035, 43811 using GC–MS. Chloroform, toluene and acetone were purchased from Fischer Scientific and used without any further purification.

The GQDs were synthesized from our previously reported method [17] as shown in Fig. S2. The Styrofoam waste was collected from appliances packaging box and cut into small pieces. These small pieces were collapsed into chloroform to extract the air trapped inside Styrofoam. The chloroform was then recovered using rota-evaporator leaving a solid mass. The solid mass of 0.5±0.01 g was pyrolyzed in a microwave (MW) furnace at ramp rate of 10 °C/min and as the temperature reaches to 390 °C, the MW furnace was turned off. A residue of dark brown color was dissolved out in toluene and filtered using a Whatman 125 mm pore grade-1 filter paper to separate out the bulk impurities. The colloidal dispersion of GQDs in toluene was used for further characterization.

The force field selection was based on the characteristics of bond breaking, bond formation and formation of new molecules in the system, similar to the experimental conditions. The ReaxFF force field was selected to carry out all the MD simulations. During the simulation, bonds can be formed and broken with reactive force field, which is an empirical potential based bond order [37,38]. The pyrolysis of hydrocarbon fuels [39,40], mechanical properties of graphene [41], CNTs [42], fullerene [42,43], ceramics [44] and polymers [45] are some of the many systems that have been successfully evaluated by using ReaxFF force field. For every system simulated in the ReaxFF, the total energy associated can be described as the summation of bond energy, lone pair energy, under-coordination stability, over-coordination energy penalty, valance angle energy, van der Waals energy, Coulomb energy, torsion angle energy and restrain energy [36].

The bond order was determined and updated at each iteration based on the interatomic distances between carbon-carbon and carbon-hydrogen atoms. The connectivity interactions like torsion and angle were also depended on bond order. A seamless transition to zero energy contribution as a result of bond breaking was ensured by the bond order dependence. The nonbonded interactions were estimated between every pair of atoms regardless of their connectedness, in contrast to nonreactive force fields. The discontinuity in nonbonded interaction energies was eliminated by taper function, while the excessive nonbonded interactions were removed by employing shielding term in van der Waals and Coulomb energy components. The complete information about energy terms included in reactive force field was given by R. Smith et al. [46].

In the work, a unit cell with periodic boundary conditions in all the three dimensions was opted for approximating an infinite system. The structure of polystyrene molecule was made by Materialsquare online molecule drawing tool (Materials Square, https://www.materialssquare.com/). The unit cells were then filled with polystyrene molecule with two different degree of polymerization (DP = 3 and 5). The energies of polystyrene structure was stabilized by using neighbour and minimize commands in LAMMPS. The simulation experiments were carried out under NVT (constant number of atoms, volume and temperature) ensemble. The time and temperature unit style was real and given as femtosecond (fs) and kelvin (K), respectively. The kinetic energy, potential energy, and position of atoms (in x, y, and z direction) were calculated by keeping atom style as charge.

The polystyrene molecule of DP 3 and 5 were kept inside a unit box with periodic boundaries. Before directly coming to the required temperature, the molecule was given some relaxation by applying neighbour, neighbour_modify, min_style, and minimize commands with the values of 2.0 bin, every 10 delay 0 check yes, cg, and 1.00e-30, 1.00e-30, 100000, 1000000, respectively. The reset_timestep was used at the end of relaxation commands to reset the timestep values to 0. After executing all the relaxation commands, ReaxFF simulations were performed at 660 K as it was the GQDs synthesis experiment actual temperature. The fix command was used to perform NVT simulation with a starting and ending temperature of 660 K and a damping factor value of 10–100 fs. Furthermore, the timestep command value was set from 0.1 to 1.5 fs depending on the simulation assessment. A gaussian function was used for the velocity of atoms with a random number. This random number was changed and used to get various similar results during all the simulations. Finally, the run and dump commands were used to run the simulation and get the calculated data. The dump file was formatted as trajectory (.traj) which included the x, y, z positions of atoms and energy values of the system. The complete LAMMPS script has been shown in the supplementary information.

The magenta, yellow and cyan pigments present in printer cartridge were cleaned using deionized water several times followed by cleaning with acetone at the end to remove the individual colour pigments and ensuring their complete extraction and isolation. The cartridge was then dried in oven for 24 h at 70 °C. After drying, 2 ml of GQDs colloidal solution in toluene was filled into cartridge pigment holes using a 5 ml syringe. The cartridge was inserted into printer and used for the application part.

The microwave furnace used in the synthesis was having four magnetrons with 1.1 kW power at 2.45 Ghz frequency. Styrofoam waste was characterized by PerkinElmer Clarus 680 GC–MS. The high resolution image of synthesized GQDs sample was collected by FEI Tecnai TF20 HR-TEM at 200 kV. The synthesized sample was also characterized using Horiba Raman spectroscopy. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy analysis was performed by Thermo Electron Scientific model with a scan speed of 64 scans/min and accumulation of 32 scans in ATR (Attenuated total reflectance) mode. GQDs were deposited on glass coverslip for X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis using Physical Electronics PHI 5000 VersaProbe III instrument. The energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) of the sample was analyzed by Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM Tescan Magna) via an EDAX Ametek detector. Hewlett-Packard (HP) inkjet (model 1200) color printer was used for printing secure message on white paper as shown in Fig. S3. LAMMPS, an opensource molecular dynamic simulation software was used for all the reactive force field simulations. Materialsquare online molecule drawing tool was used to prepare polystyrene structures for LAMMPS simulations. An opensource software, Ovito was used to see all the results and convert data into other formats. All the 3D models in the publication were prepared using Blender.
GQDs structure evolution from polystyrene molecule was discovered by MD simulations and compared with the experimental results.

The GQDs synthesized using this method was nonpolar in nature and showed its solubility in nonpolar solvents like toluene, chloroform and cyclohexane. The size of the GQDs was 5.5±1.5 nm as confirmed by using high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) shown in Fig. 1 A. The d spacing between carbon-carbon atoms was also calculated by using fringes data of high resolution image showed in Fig. S4. The 0.24 nm d spacing indicated the presence of graphene like structure with sp2 hybridization of carbon atoms. The synthesized GQDs also showed strong G (graphitic) band with less intensity D (defects) band, indicated single layered graphene like structure with minimal defects as confirmed using Raman spectroscopy in Fig. 1B.

The FTIR spectroscopy was performed for Styrofoam waste and synthesized GQDs samples for the determination of functional groups as shown in Fig. 2 . The waste Styrofoam specimen exhibited identical characteristic spectral peaks to those observed in Thermo Fisher Scientific's reference material library for Styrofoam. In contrast, the GQDs sample exhibited distinct spectral features, characterized by two prominent peaks observed at wavenumbers of 956 cm−1 and 1410 cm−1. These spectral signatures are indicative of C = C bending vibrations and C = C aromatic stretching modes, respectively [47]. Moreover, the XPS full scan survey was performed for GQDs sample showed presence of carbon element as shown in Fig. 2B. The high-resolution C1s spectrum confirmed the presence of the carbon sp2 hybridization peak at an energy level of 282.7 electron volts (eV) [17]. Also, the absence of discernible peak shoulders precluded the possibility of deconvolution for subsequent analytical. The absence of sp3 hybridization peak at 286 eV, confirmed the complete conversion of Styrofoam waste into GQDs [48]. The presence of carbon and absence of any other elements in GQDs sample was also confirmed using EDS as shown in Fig. S5. Hence, the presence of carbon with sp2 hybridization and absence of any hydroxyl or carboxyl functional groups were confirmed by EDS, XPS and FTIR spectroscopies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evolution of cyclic structure from polystyrene
ce:section.ce:para: First, the investigations of intermediate species were performed to understand the synthesis mechanism properly. Polystyrene molecule with DP of three and five as shown in Fig. 3 A and B were taken in a periodic boundary of three dimensional box. In a typical thermal simulation, the vibration of molecules decide the overall temperature of the system and a temperature of 660 K matching with the experimental conditions was applied inside the simulation box. The damping factor of 100 fs was used to control the under and overshoot of the temperature in the system with a timestep value of 1.5 fs. The velocity with a random number was also used to know the different possibilities by keeping all other parameters same. The position of all the atoms in x, y, and z direction was calculated and stored in a trajectory file, used for further analysis.

The position data was extracted from the trajectory file and individual atoms were drawn by Blender 3D modelling software. Fig. 4 A showed, the formation of a cyclic ring structure (shown in red color) from polystyrene molecule after applying the temperature. The red color cyclic structure had no hydrogen atoms attached to it. Further, by changing the random number used in velocity and keeping all other parameters same, gave interesting results. On changing the random number, a cyclic ring structure with one or more hydrogen atoms was formed as shown in Fig. 4B. It was also observed that the placement of hydrogen atom on cyclic structure was not fixed and depended on the random number value of velocity parameter. Interestingly, a benzene radical structure formation was also observed with five hydrogen atoms attached to cyclic structure. The formation of benzene radical by thermal treatment of polystyrene has been already reported by many.[49,50] The gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) study further confirmed the formation of cyclic structure with five or less hydrogen atoms attached to it,[51] as also output by the current simulation study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of GQDs from cyclic structure
ce:section.ce:para: The cyclic structure having one or more hydrogen formed in the previous step were taken in adequate number in a three-dimensional box (Fig. 5 A). The simulation setup was again set at a temperature of 660 K similar to the experimental conditions. A timestep value and damping factor of 0.1 fs and 10 fs were used respectively to control the fluctuations in the temperature. A 3D model (Fig. 5B) and the vi. deo (Movie S1) were created using result’s trajectory file containing the position and direction of all the atoms. The model depicted a hexagon graphene like structure formation containing few pentagon rings. Interestingly, it was also observed that hydrogen atoms (shown in red color) in between the graphene structure created hindrance, which resulted in graphene structural defects in GQDs [52–55].

Furthermore, cyclic structure without any hydrogen atom was also tested to get better understanding of synthesis mechanism. Few cyclic structures without hydrogen atoms were taken in the periodic boundary simulation box as show in Fig. 5C. The temperature, timestep, and damping factor were adjusted to 660 K, 0.1 fs, and 10 fs respectively, same as the above simulation conditions. The results in Fig. 5D showed the formation of graphene like structure purely based on carbon atoms arranged in hexagon pattern. No pentagon impurity were observed as compare to cyclic structure with random carbon atoms simulation performed in Fig. 4B.

Moreover, the formation of GQDs, degradation of cyclic ring structure, and intermediate species were also analysed. In case of cyclic ring structure with hydrogen atoms (Fig. 6 A), GQDs were formed with some intermediate species. Some fluctuations in the graph were observed in case of intermediate species, as few cyclic structures were forming and degrading at the same time. GQDs structure was formed in the simulation box after 40 ns by using 16 cyclic structures with random hydrogen atoms around. On the other hand in Fig. 6B, the cyclic structures without any hydrogen atom led to the formation of GQDs without any defects. Also, no intermediate species were observed at the end of simulation.

Based on the simulation results and analysis of GQDs obtained from pyrolysis of Styrofoam, following mechanism (scheme 1 ) was proposed. Several reactive intermediates, bearing saturated and unsaturated six carbon cyclic structures were formed upon pyrolysis of polystyrene chains, which then rearranged to develop a graphitic structure.

The developed cost-effective technique not only address nonbiodegradable waste management but can also have great impact in anti-counterfeiting applications. The synthesized GQDs exhibited a strong fluorescent making them highly suitable for security ink formulations. The colloidal solution of GQDs in toluene was prepared and loaded into printer cartridge. The solution served as the ink for printing secure messages on white A4-size paper using an HP printer. By leveraging the exceptional fluorescence of GQDs, the printed message was invisible to naked eyes under normal lighting conditions as shown in Fig. 7 A. However, upon exposure to UV light of 350 nm, the printed message exhibited a discernible visible appearance, as depicted in Fig. 6B. The scientific approach ensured the secure message remained hidden to the naked eyes but can be readily detected and authenticated using a UV light.

The formation mechanism of GQDs from microwave pyrolysis of Styrofoam waste was investigated using reactive force field (ReaxFF) simulation. It was found that GQDs structure started forming at 660 K temperature, matching with the experimental conditions. The Styrofoam was first converted into cyclic structure attached with five or less hydrogen atoms bonded at random positions. The combination of these cyclic structures led to the formation of an intermediate species that had two or more cyclic structures bonded together. The larger graphene-like structure was created by merging the intermediate species which resulted in the formation of GQDs. Interestingly, few carbon pentagons rings were also formed with hexagons in case of hydrogen attached cyclic structure, while in the case of cyclic structures without any hydrogen atoms, pure hexagon graphene like structure was observed. The formation of carbon pentagons as impurity in the overall hexagonal structure was plausibly evolved due to the hindrance created by the hydrogen atoms. Thus, it could be confirmed that the results of simulation studies were aligned with the experimental conditions. The approach may further be used to study the formation of other carbon-nanomaterials including graphene, graphene oxide, and CNTs. Moreover, exceptional optical properties of GQDs hold a great scientific promise in the field of security inks. The nonbiodegradable waste-based synthesis of GQDs could open new cost-effective opportunities in the field of anti-counterfeiting applications. The current approach enables the development of security prints with enhanced counterfeit detection and authentication capabilities. Functionalization of GQDs with different moieties to tune its polarity and optical properties may also be explored using the ReaxFF simulation condition in future.

Dheeraj Kumar: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investiga, tion, Writing – original draft. Blesson Tom Mathew: Data curation, Formal analysis. Sweety Rani: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Advitiya Kumar: Data curation, Formal analysis. Bhanu Nandan: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Rajiv K. Srivastava: Conceptualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by the Indian Institute of Technology Delhi and the Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India (Dy. No. C/5142/IFD) to perform this research work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109681.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 2

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-Pot Synthesis of Fluorescent Carbon Dots from Orange Waste Peels
Ind Eng Chem Res

Mater Lett

New Journal of Chemistry

Chem Res Chin Univ

J Fluoresc

RSC Adv

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of Carbon Quantum Dot Nanoparticles Derived from Byproducts in Bio-Refinery Process for Cell Imaging and In Vivo Bioimaging
